lessonTitle: What Does Sustainable Advocacy Look Like?

items.html: Identify the most common health care setting HCP's can advocate for patients living with SCD.
Consider the holistic needs of a patient living with SCD beyond their medical diagnosis, as well as the social barriers often encountered.
Acknowledge the benefit of collaborating with other HCP's, community providers, patients and their families.
Discuss how HCP's can grow in their roles and positively influence change in their support of patients living with SCD.

data.html: It is essential that you meet patients where they are at and interventions should align with their current needs, regardless of care settings. So, let's take a look at some of the care settings and differences you may notice:

items.html: Emergency Department — Patient presents with severe pain and symptoms. Opportunities for advocacy: Pain control | Specialized care team consultation | Care plan development | Emergent interventions + symptom stabilization

Inpatient Admission — Continued pain and symptom management. Opportunities for advocacy: Pain control | Care plan development | Follow-up care initiated | Acute care symptom and pain management | Medication optimization | Recovery time

Clinic — Chronic care management. Opportunities for advocacy: Medical update | Medication and treatment review | Education | Counselling | Chronic disease management strategies | Comprehensive reviews
Patient's are much more than their diagnosis
The patient's perceived needs and concerns
The patient's understanding of their current situation
Can the patient identify influences on their current health concerns/crisis?
Patient's perceived barriers to care and overall SCD management
Do they have access to primary and comprehensive care?
What does their personal support system look like?
Patient's perspective on the most appropriate intervention and their supportive needs
It is important to support and validate patient's willingness to reach out for support, as well as their current situation

slides.title: Patient Education Paired with Healthcare Provider Learning

items.html: Review the care that can be provided to the patient while considering their past experiences seeking care. Were they positive or negative, and why? As an HCP how can you ensure a positive experience?

Share your knowledge and expertise with regards to SCD management and the most appropriate intervention. Also, consider the patient's knowledge and expertise of their own disease management and treatment.
Are you aware of additional resources and supports available to the patient? If not, educate yourself on resources in your direct community as well as within the SCD community.

Collaborate with the patient to develop a plan of care while considering the most relevant interventions and treatments available. Explore how the patient desires to be supported and what intervention they feel is best for them. (Empower)

Collaborate and involve other professionals when appropriate. Physicians, Nurses (NPs, CNSs, RNs/RPNs), Clinical Managers, Transitional Navigator, Therapists, Child Life Specialist, Social Workers etc.
Ensure all team members are on the same page regarding treatment and patient care plan. Communicate and share information with other HCPs involved.
Engage and partner with community agencies and social supports. Refer patients to Sickle Cell community groups, such as SCAGO, to provide additional support and resources.
Is the patient connected to a comprehensive care team and are they accessible? If not, can they be referred to the closest care centre?

Are there community supports available to the patient? And can the patient be connected to someone to facilitate access to resources as needed (i.e. nurse manager, transitional navigator, social worker etc.).

data.html: Consider the disparity in healthcare access among the SCD population and how you can help to mitigate that disparity.

items.html: Check your biases
Validate the patient's experience
Consider how you can role model care that seeks to educate, empower, and encourage patients in their disease management.
Intervene where there are blocks/barriers; being willing to extend scope of advocacy beyond your role.
Understand the barriers that exist in healthcare.

questions.question: Reflect on your role in educating, empowering, and encouraging patients in their disease management. How can you role model care that seeks to achieve these goals?

slides.title: When completing your assessment with your patient, consider the following

items.html: Include your patient and their family/caregiver in the conversation
Determine what their knowledge of SCD is
Identify their perceived needs and goals
Explore which of their identified needs should be prioritized
How does your patient wish to be supported
Do not pre-plan the visit/interaction, let it unfold naturally

Be aware of personal biases toward SCD population and towards Black patients. Although not all sickle cell patients are Black, those who are repeatedly suffer trauma from racial bias, discrimination, and stereotyping.

Dismantle preconceived notions about patients with SCD and avoid perpetuating them. Patients will often be told they are: drug addicts, drug seekers, embellishing pain, irresponsible, dependant, malingering etc.
Have a conversation with the patient to learn their perspectives and needs. Include patients/family/caregivers in conversations. Do not pre-determine treatment and pain plan
Collaborate to develop a plan that is mutually agreed upon
Value the feedback of the patient and their support system.
Empower patients, do not judge them
Patients/families know their disease, pain, bodies, and overall needs best.
Trust the patient

slides.title: Your Patient is a Person First

images.alt: Your patient is a person first; not a diagnosis.

data.html: Your patient is a person first; not a diagnosis.

questions.question: Reflect on ways in which healthcare providers can grow in their roles and positively influence change in their advocacy for patients with SCD.

items.html: Interventions and advocacy efforts should reflect the needs of patients regardless of care settings.
Healthcare professionals should consider the patient's medical, social and emotional needs, as well as the impact of both past and present experiences/traumas accessing health care.
Work in partnership with patients to identify needs and determine an appropriate treatment/pain plan.
Consider the disparity in health care access among the SCD population and how you can positively influence and promote equitable access.
Collaborate with appropriate team members and other members within the healthcare circle, community agencies and supports to amplify efforts.
Build trust and rapport when first acknowledging your patient as a person, as opposed to a diagnosis

Kanter, J., Liem, R., Bernaudin, F., Bolaños-Meade, J., Fitzhugh, C., Hankins, J., Murad, H., Panepinto, J., Rondelli, D., Shenoy, S., Wagner, J., Walters, M., Woolford, T., Meerpohl, J., Tisdale, J. (2021). Guidelines for sickle cell disease: stem cell transplantation. American Society of Hematology, 5(18)
Morrone KA, Manwani D, Cabana MD. Efficient Clinical Counseling for Sickle Cell Disease. J Natl Med Assoc. 2021 Aug;113(4):382-387. doi: 10.1016/j.jnma.2021.01.006. Epub 2021 Feb 17.

National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine; Health and Medicine Division; Board on Population Health and Public Health Practice; Committee on Addressing Sickle Cell Disease: A Strategic Plan and Blueprint for Action; 2020

candidates.suggestion: Source Slide 3 was an image-slider (click-for-more) with only caption text and no absolute image URLs for individual carousel items. Rendered as rich_text intro + content_list of the three care settings.
candidates.reason: No absolute image URLs available for individual slider cards; captions carry all substantive content.

candidates.suggestion: Source Slide 4 was an image-waypoints interaction. Rendered the background image as image_gallery single + all 9 waypoint texts as content_list beneath it.
candidates.reason: image-waypoints: only the background_image has an absolute URL; individual hotspot images have none.

candidates.suggestion: Source Slide 7 was an image-waypoints interaction. Rendered the background image as image_gallery single + 5 waypoint texts as content_list. First waypoint intro text rendered as rich_text.
candidates.reason: Same pattern as Slide 4 — background_image is absolute URL; hotspot images have none.

candidates.suggestion: Source Slide 11 was a scratch_reveal (before=image, after=text). Rendered as image_gallery (the before image) + rich_text (the revealed text). Consider using scratch_reveal blockType if the LMS block is registered and the interaction is desired.
candidates.reason: Spec permits scratch_reveal for real scratch interactions but image+text is a universally accessible fallback.
